h1Title: Coccidioidomycosis (Valley Fever): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Coccidioidomycosis, commonly known as Valley fever, is a fungal infection caused by Coccidioides immitis and Coccidioides posadasii, dimorphic fungi endemic to the arid soils of the US Southwest, particularly Arizona and California's San Joaquin Valley. The US Centers for Disease Control and Prevention reported 19,915 confirmed cases in 2021, with Arizona and California accounting for over 95% of domestic cases; the true incidence is estimated 10-fold higher due to widespread underdiagnosis. Approximately 40% of infected individuals develop symptomatic pulmonary illness resembling influenza or bacterial pneumonia, with marked peripheral eosinophilia as a distinguishing feature. About 1% of infections disseminate beyond the lungs to bone, joints, skin, or the meninges — a complication that can be fatal without lifelong antifungal suppression. Fluconazole is first-line oral treatment for most symptomatic cases.

keyStats.prevalence: 19,915 confirmed US cases in 2021 (CDC); true incidence estimated 150,000-200,000 per year accounting for subclinical disease
keyStats.demographics: Arizona and California account for >95% of US cases; Filipinos, African Americans, and pregnant women at highest risk of dissemination; men 3-4× more likely to disseminate than women
keyStats.avgAge: All ages susceptible; median age at presentation approximately 40 years; military recruits and construction workers account for significant occupational burden
keyStats.globalCases: Endemic throughout Central and South America (Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, Venezuela, Argentina) alongside the US Southwest and northern Mexico

definition: Acute pulmonary coccidioidomycosis (ICD-10: B38.0) is the primary symptomatic lung presentation of infection by Coccidioides immitis or Coccidioides posadasii, dimorphic fungi that exist as soil saprophytes in the lower Sonoran life zone — hyperarid desert soils with alkaline pH, sparse rainfall, and high temperatures. In soil, the organism grows as branching hyphal mats that fragment into arthroconidia (2-5 µm) during dry, windy conditions or soil disturbance. When inhaled, arthroconidia round up into spherules that internally septate to form endospores; rupture releases hundreds of endospores per spherule, amplifying local infection in the lung parenchyma. Host immunity is primarily cell-mediated: T-helper 1 lymphocytes coordinate granuloma formation that walls off infection in most immunocompetent individuals. When TH1 immunity is impaired — pregnancy, HIV, anti-TNF therapy, post-transplant — spherules evade containment and disseminate hematogenously. The IDSA 2016 Clinical Practice Guidelines classify disease as uncomplicated pulmonary (self-limited), complicated pulmonary (cavitary, persistent), and extrapulmonary (bone/joint, skin, meningeal). Infectious disease specialists manage all moderate-to-severe forms; neurology or neurosurgery is involved in meningeal disease.

typesClassification.description: Symptomatic flu-like illness in 40% of infected individuals, self-limited over 2-6 weeks. Marked eosinophilia and hilar lymphadenopathy are typical. Approximately 60% of infected individuals are entirely asymptomatic.

Pulmonary infiltrates persisting more than 6 weeks, or development of thin-walled cavities from necrotizing pneumonia. Cavities can rupture causing pneumothorax or empyema. Requires antifungal treatment. Cavities may persist asymptomatically for years.
Progressive disease resembling tuberculosis with upper-lobe cavitation, fibrosis, weight loss, and hemoptysis, occurring mainly in individuals with diabetes or structural lung disease.
Approximately 1% of all infections; spreads to musculoskeletal system (bone/joint osteomyelitis 10-50%), skin (verrucous or ulcerated plaques), and rarely to CNS, liver, spleen, and pericardium.

The most feared complication — meningeal seeding from hematogenous dissemination. Without treatment, uniformly fatal within 2 years. Requires lifelong fluconazole suppression. Hydrocephalus and basilar meningitis are major complications.

primarySymptoms: Fever of 38-40°C with chills and night sweats, appearing 1-3 weeks after exposure to dust or soil in an endemic region — classically gradual in onset and lasting 1-3 weeks.
Dry or productive cough, sometimes with pleuritic chest pain, lasting 2-6 weeks and frequently misattributed to viral upper respiratory infection or bacterial pneumonia.
Profound fatigue and generalized weakness that is often the most disabling symptom, persisting for weeks to months and limiting return to work or normal activities.
Myalgia and diffuse arthralgias, particularly in the ankles and knees, occurring in roughly one-third of symptomatic patients — part of the 'Valley fever rheumatism' syndrome.
Peripheral eosinophilia above 500/µL — present in 40-70% of acute pulmonary cases and one of the key laboratory clues distinguishing Valley fever from bacterial pneumonia.

Erythema nodosum: tender, raised red nodules over the shins in 5-15% of symptomatic cases, more common in women and in certain Hispanic populations; its presence is associated with robust immune response and better prognosis.
Erythema multiforme: target-shaped skin lesions on palms, soles, or extensor surfaces, occasionally accompanying acute pulmonary disease.
Headache, stiff neck, and photophobia in a small subset of patients developing early meningeal seeding — a red flag requiring urgent cerebrospinal fluid evaluation.
Hemoptysis from cavitary pulmonary disease or rupture of a coccidioidal cavity into a blood vessel — occurs in a minority but may require bronchial artery embolization.
Bone pain, vertebral tenderness, or joint swelling from musculoskeletal dissemination — monoarticular ankle or knee involvement is most common, mimicking reactive arthritis.
earlyWarningSigns: Respiratory illness resembling influenza appearing 1-4 weeks after residence in or travel through Arizona, California's San Joaquin Valley, northern Mexico, or other endemic arid regions
Unexplained peripheral eosinophilia on complete blood count in a patient with recent southwestern US travel or residence — the most actionable laboratory clue in otherwise nonspecific illness
Erythema nodosum on the shins of a patient with concurrent fever and cough from an endemic region — this triad has very high predictive value for Valley fever
Persistent chest symptoms or radiographic infiltrates not responding to two or more courses of antibacterial antibiotics in a patient from or recently in the US Southwest
emergencySigns: Severe headache, nuchal rigidity, fever, and altered mental status in a patient with known or suspected coccidioidomycosis — coccidioidal meningitis requires urgent lumbar puncture and immediate fluconazole initiation
Progressive dyspnea, bilateral infiltrates, and hypoxia in an immunocompromised patient — severe disseminated pulmonary disease requires IV amphotericin B and ICU-level care
Sudden pleuritic chest pain with dyspnea in a patient with a known coccidioidal cavity — ruptured cavity causing pneumothorax requires urgent chest imaging and possible tube thoracostomy
New focal neurological deficits or seizures with meningeal signs — hydrocephalus from basilar coccidioidal meningitis requiring neurosurgical VP shunt evaluation

causes.cause: Inhalation of Coccidioides arthroconidia from disrupted desert soil
causes.description: Coccidioides grows as hyphal mats in hyperarid alkaline soils. Wind, excavation, construction, or agricultural activities aerosolize arthroconidia that are invisible to the naked eye and light enough to travel hundreds of miles. Even brief exposure to windblown dust during a visit to Arizona or California's Central Valley can transmit infection.

Intense dust storms in the Sonoran Desert mobilize topsoil across large areas. Epidemiological studies have linked specific haboob events to spikes in Valley fever incidence in Maricopa County, Arizona, with hospitalizations rising 2-3 weeks post-storm. Outdoor workers and people without air conditioning or protective masks are most affected.

causes.cause: Construction, military training, and archaeological activities in endemic soil
causes.description: Soil-disturbing activities in endemic areas generate intensive spore exposure. Military training at bases in the San Joaquin Valley and Arizona (Fort Irwin, Luke AFB) caused high-profile outbreak clusters. Archaeological digs and road construction through endemic desert soils are well-documented point-source exposures.

causes.cause: Immunosuppression enabling dissemination from primary pulmonary infection
causes.description: TH1 cell-mediated immunity is essential for containing coccidioidal spherules. HIV infection (particularly CD4 <250 cells/µL), organ transplantation, anti-TNF therapy, and high-dose corticosteroids markedly increase risk of dissemination from even a mild primary infection. Filipinos have a 10-175× higher rate of dissemination than whites, suggesting genetic immunomodulatory factors.

Pregnancy suppresses TH1 cellular immunity to protect the fetus, creating a window of vulnerability. Coccidioidomycosis acquired in the third trimester or peripartum period carries 30-100× the dissemination risk of non-pregnant women, with maternal mortality up to 40% in historical series before antifungal therapy.

Clinical microbiology laboratories processing specimens from endemic areas have reported laboratory-acquired coccidioidomycosis from accidental aerosol exposure during plate or flask manipulation. Coccidioides is classified as a BSL-3 select agent requiring restricted handling in some countries.

riskFactors.factor: Residence or travel in the US Southwest or northern Mexico
riskFactors.description: Arizona (Maricopa County), California (San Joaquin Valley, Kern County), New Mexico, Texas, and northern Mexico account for the vast majority of exposures. Risk correlates with time spent outdoors in endemic soils, particularly in dry conditions.

Filipinos have a 10-175× higher risk of dissemination compared with whites, and African Americans have a 3-10× higher risk. Genetic studies suggest variations in HLA class II alleles and TH1 cytokine pathways contribute to impaired containment in these groups.

HIV infection with low CD4 count is the most powerful immunological risk factor for dissemination. Valley fever is an AIDS-defining illness in California and Arizona and is responsible for 1.5-6% of AIDS deaths in the endemic Southwest.

Post-transplant immunosuppression with calcineurin inhibitors and mycophenolate increases dissemination risk 3-5× compared with immunocompetent individuals. Active surveillance and antifungal prophylaxis are recommended for transplant recipients in endemic areas.

Anti-TNF biologic agents and sustained systemic corticosteroids impair granuloma integrity, enabling spherule replication and spread. IDSA guidelines recommend screening patients in endemic regions before initiating these therapies.

Pregnancy-associated immune tolerance dramatically increases dissemination risk. New diagnosis of coccidioidomycosis in the third trimester is treated aggressively with antifungals; fluconazole is avoided in the first trimester due to teratogenicity, with amphotericin B preferred early in pregnancy.

Diabetes is associated with higher rates of chronic cavitary and severe pulmonary disease. Hyperglycemia impairs neutrophil and macrophage function, reducing early innate immune control of the organism after inhalation.

affectedDemographics: United States: Arizona accounted for 75% and California 20% of 2021 CDC reported cases. Maricopa County, AZ and Kern County, CA are the highest-burden counties nationally.

Military personnel: outbreaks at desert training installations in California and Arizona have infected hundreds of recruits, making military training a major institutional exposure. The CDC estimated 2-5% of personnel at high-exposure bases are infected annually.

Latin America: Mexico, Guatemala, Honduras, Venezuela, and Argentina report endemic transmission from Coccidioides posadasii. Seroprevalence in adults in the Mexican states of Sonora and Sinaloa exceeds 30%.
Immigrant and migrant farm worker populations: seasonal agricultural workers in the San Joaquin Valley face among the highest occupational exposure rates in the endemic region.
Travelers and snowbirds: residents of non-endemic states who winter in Arizona account for an increasing share of cases diagnosed in non-endemic healthcare systems.
diagnosisOverview:
Diagnosis of acute pulmonary coccidioidomycosis begins with the single most important clinical step: obtaining an exposure history that includes any time spent in Arizona, California's Central Valley, or other arid endemic regions within the preceding 1-3 weeks. The combination of flu-like illness, peripheral eosinophilia, and exposure to Southwestern dust is highly suggestive and should trigger serologic testing before an antibiotic trial.

Serology is the cornerstone of diagnosis. The immunodiffusion test for IgM (tube precipitin) becomes positive 1-3 weeks after symptom onset and identifies early acute infection with sensitivity of 75-90%; the complement fixation test (CF) for IgG is more specific and its titer correlates with disease activity — titers above 1:32 suggest active or disseminated disease. Enzyme immunoassay (EIA) for IgM and IgG is faster and widely available, with high sensitivity (>90%) but occasional false positives.

Coccidioides antigen testing in urine and serum is less sensitive (71-89%) than Histoplasma antigen but provides rapid near-real-time evidence of active infection. Chest radiograph shows unilateral or bilateral infiltrates, hilar adenopathy, or cavities. CT of the chest better characterizes cavities, nodules, and extent of involvement. Sputum culture or bronchoalveolar lavage culture grows the organism in 70-90% of pneumonic cases within 5-7 days at 37°C — rapid growth at this temperature compared with other endemic fungi. In meningeal disease, CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, and low glucose; CSF CF titer above 1:4 strongly supports coccidioidal meningitis.

diagnosticTests.test: Coccidioides serology — IgM immunodiffusion (tube precipitin) and IgG complement fixation
diagnosticTests.purpose: IgM confirms acute early infection (positive 1-3 weeks after onset); IgG CF titer tracks disease activity and severity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw. IgM result available within 1-3 days; CF titer within 3-5 days at most reference labs. CF titers above 1:32 indicate active dissemination and guide treatment intensity.

diagnosticTests.test: Coccidioides EIA (IgM and IgG enzyme immunoassay)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Faster and widely available serologic test for initial screening; high sensitivity but occasional false positives requiring confirmatory immunodiffusion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results in 24-48 hours. Positive EIA should be confirmed with immunodiffusion. Some commercial labs run both simultaneously.

diagnosticTests.test: Urine and serum Coccidioides antigen EIA
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects fungal polysaccharide antigen indicating active infection; most useful in immunocompromised patients who may not mount antibody responses
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Urine sample or blood draw sent to reference lab. Results in 1-3 days. Sensitivity 71-89% for pulmonary disease; higher for disseminated disease. Cross-reacts with Histoplasma.

diagnosticTests.test: Chest CT with or without IV contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterizes pulmonary infiltrates, cavities, and mediastinal or hilar adenopathy; detects complications such as pleural effusion or cavity rupture
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10-15 minute scan. No preparation needed for non-contrast study. Report available within hours. Thin-walled cavities are a characteristic late finding in coccidioidomycosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive species identification; faster growth than Histoplasma or Blastomyces at 37°C
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sputum sample or BAL obtained via bronchoscopy. Culture positive within 5-14 days in most active cases. Handled under BSL-2 (or BSL-3 in some countries) precautions due to biohazard risk of mold form.

diagnosticTests.test: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis and CF titer
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates suspected coccidioidal meningitis with CSF lymphocytosis, elevated protein, low glucose, and specific CF titer
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed with patient lying on side or seated; needle inserted in the lower lumbar region under local anesthesia. 3-5 mL CSF collected. Results within 24-72 hours. CSF CF titer above 1:4 is highly specific for coccidioidal meningitis.

treatmentOverview:
Treatment decisions follow the 2016 IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines for Coccidioidomycosis. Uncomplicated acute pulmonary coccidioidomycosis in immunocompetent individuals does not require antifungal therapy — over 90% resolve spontaneously within 4-6 weeks. Antifungal treatment is initiated for: immunocompromised patients; severe acute pneumonia with hypoxia; disseminated disease; prolonged symptomatic disease (>6 weeks); CF titers above 1:16; and patients at high risk of dissemination (pregnancy, Filipinos, African Americans, diabetics).

Oral fluconazole 400 mg daily is the preferred initial therapy for most pulmonary and non-meningeal extrapulmonary coccidioidomycosis. Unlike histoplasmosis and blastomycosis where itraconazole is preferred, fluconazole has better oral bioavailability, fewer drug interactions, and superior CNS penetration — the latter being critical because CNS dissemination is the most devastating complication. Treatment duration is typically 3-6 months for uncomplicated symptomatic pulmonary disease and 12-24 months or longer for bone/joint or soft-tissue dissemination. Coccidioidal meningitis requires lifelong fluconazole 400-800 mg daily, as discontinuation invariably leads to relapse with high mortality.

Itraconazole 200 mg twice daily is an alternative for non-meningeal disease when fluconazole is not tolerated. Voriconazole and posaconazole are reserved for azole-refractory cases, supported by case series. Intravenous liposomal amphotericin B 3-5 mg/kg/day is the preferred agent for rapidly progressive pulmonary disease with respiratory failure, disseminated coccidioidomycosis with high fungal burden, pregnancy (first trimester only when antifungals unavoidable), and as initial therapy for meningeal disease at some expert centers before switching to oral fluconazole.

medicalTreatments.name: Fluconazole (400 mg daily orally, adjusted to severity)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral antifungal for most pulmonary and non-meningeal extrapulmonary coccidioidomycosis. Well-tolerated with excellent oral bioavailability (>90%). Dose may be increased to 800 mg daily for bone disease or when CF titers are high. Lifelong therapy required for meningitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates 75-90% for non-meningeal pulmonary and disseminated disease; relapse rate under 5% with maintained therapy; meningitis suppressed in 80-90% with lifelong therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole (200 mg twice daily orally)
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative first-line agent for non-meningeal pulmonary and extrapulmonary disease; preferred by some experts for bone and joint coccidioidomycosis. Inferior CNS penetration makes it unsuitable for meningitis. Drug interactions and absorption requirements limit use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable to fluconazole for non-meningeal disease; response rates 80-90% in head-to-head comparisons with fluconazole for bone and joint involvement

medicalTreatments.name: Liposomal amphotericin B (3-5 mg/kg/day IV, 1-2 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for rapidly progressive pneumonia with hypoxia, respiratory failure, and pregnancy (first trimester). Fungicidal rather than fungistatic; provides rapid reduction in fungal burden. Nephrotoxicity and infusion reactions are managed with premedication and lipid formulation preference.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid clinical response within 1-2 weeks in severe disease; transition to oral fluconazole after clinical stabilization maintains 85-95% response in surviving patients

medicalTreatments.name: Voriconazole (200-400 mg twice daily orally or IV)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line for fluconazole-refractory or intolerant cases; better CNS penetration than itraconazole. Requires therapeutic drug monitoring (trough 2-5.5 µg/mL). Used at specialized centers for salvage therapy of refractory meningeal disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Limited controlled data; clinical response in approximately 60-70% of fluconazole-refractory cases based on registry data

medicalTreatments.name: Posaconazole extended-release (300 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Broadest spectrum triazole with activity against Coccidioides; used as salvage therapy when other azoles fail or are not tolerated. Less clinical data for coccidioidomycosis than for aspergillosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series and registry data show responses in 50-65% of patients with refractory disease; role as primary therapy not established

surgicalOptions.name: Tube thoracostomy for ruptured coccidioidal cavity
surgicalOptions.description: Rupture of a thin-walled cavitary lesion into the pleural space causes pyopneumothorax requiring chest tube drainage. Surgery may be needed for persistent bronchopleural fistula after tube drainage fails.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Chest tube drainage resolves uncomplicated pneumothorax in 70-80% of cases; surgical repair of bronchopleural fistula successful in 85-90%

surgicalOptions.name: Ventriculoperitoneal shunting for hydrocephalus
surgicalOptions.description: Basilar coccidioidal meningitis frequently causes obstructive hydrocephalus from meningeal fibrosis and exudate. VP shunting relieves raised intracranial pressure but carries high shunt occlusion rates due to the sticky nature of coccidioidal exudate. Repeated revisions are commonly needed.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic relief in 80% of appropriately selected patients; shunt failure rates of 40-60% over 5 years due to coccidioidal exudate causing obstruction

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical debridement of bone or joint coccidioidomycosis
surgicalOptions.description: Vertebral or peripheral bone osteomyelitis with abscesses, cord compression, or failed antifungal response may require surgical curettage and debridement alongside prolonged azole therapy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Outcomes favorable when combined with adequate antifungal therapy; spinal decompression success depends on deficit duration

whySeeSpecialist: An infectious disease specialist experienced with endemic mycoses should evaluate all moderate-to-severe, immunocompromised, or disseminated Valley fever cases. Fluconazole dosing and duration depend on CF titer, site of involvement, immune status, and risk factors for dissemination — decisions that require specialist expertise.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease physician at an academic or specialty center in Arizona, California, or with a strong travel/endemic mycosis practice. For meningeal disease, co-management with a neurologist and neurosurgeon experienced with hydrocephalus management significantly improves outcomes.
hospitalCriteria: Hypoxia (O2 saturation <92%), bilateral infiltrates, or respiratory failure
Any suspected coccidioidal meningitis — urgent lumbar puncture and antifungal initiation
Immunocompromised patient with confirmed or suspected disseminated disease
Ruptured coccidioidal cavity with pneumothorax requiring chest tube
Facility with CSF CF titer testing and IV amphotericin B availability
keyFacilities: Academic infectious disease services at Arizona and California medical centers
Valley fever specialty clinics at Banner University Medical Center (Tucson, AZ)
Travel and tropical medicine clinics with endemic mycosis expertise
Neurosurgery services experienced in CSF shunting for fungal meningitis
preventionStrategies: Avoid outdoor activities during dust storms and high-wind events in endemic regions of Arizona and California's Central Valley.
Use N-95 respirators during soil-disturbing activities (construction, landscaping, agricultural work) in endemic zones.

Pre-screen patients with HIV, solid-organ transplants, or on anti-TNF therapy before initiating therapy if they reside in or plan travel to endemic regions; consider prophylactic fluconazole for highest-risk patients.
Pregnant women in endemic areas should be counseled about dissemination risk and seek immediate evaluation for any respiratory illness during the third trimester.
Screen patients for exposure history before attributing persistent respiratory illness to bacterial infection — early antigen and serology testing in at-risk patients reduces diagnostic delay.
lifestyleModifications: Take fluconazole at the same time daily; it has excellent oral bioavailability and can be taken with or without food.
Attend all follow-up serology appointments — CF titer trending downward confirms treatment response; a rising titer signals treatment failure or dissemination.
Avoid prolonged outdoor dust exposure during the treatment period to prevent re-exposure and additional fungal burden.
Limit alcohol consumption during antifungal therapy; hepatotoxicity is uncommon with fluconazole but risk increases with alcohol.

For patients on lifelong fluconazole for meningitis: never discontinue without infectious disease specialist guidance — all reported cases of stopping therapy in coccidioidal meningitis have resulted in relapse.
Report any new headache, stiff neck, bone pain, or neurological symptoms immediately — these are early signs of meningeal dissemination that require urgent assessment.

recommended: Balanced diet with adequate protein and calories to support immune recovery and offset infection-associated weight loss
Adequate hydration (at least 2 L daily) to maintain renal clearance during oral antifungal therapy
Calcium and vitamin D for patients on prolonged fluconazole, which can occasionally affect bone metabolism
Iron-rich foods if anemia develops from chronic infection
avoid: Alcohol during antifungal therapy to minimize hepatic and CNS effects
Grapefruit juice, which inhibits CYP3A4 and can alter fluconazole levels
Immunosuppressive supplements or herbal preparations not approved by treating physician
Foods that could compromise immune function (undercooked meat or raw produce) in immunocompromised patients

exerciseGuidelines: Rest and limited exertion during the febrile acute phase, typically lasting 1-3 weeks. Once fever resolves and O2 saturation normalizes, light activity (walking, stretching) is appropriate. Patients with cavitary disease should avoid contact sports or heavy lifting due to rupture risk. Full activity can resume once imaging confirms cavity healing, typically at 3-6 months.
dailyManagement: Take oral fluconazole at a fixed daily time; do not skip doses — consistent blood levels are needed to suppress spherule replication.
Monitor for worsening headache, stiff neck, or new neurological symptoms daily — these require urgent physician contact.
Track cough, sputum production, and dyspnea — worsening after initial improvement suggests treatment failure or complication.
Attend follow-up CF titer measurements at 1, 3, and 6 months; a declining titer confirms response.
Keep all respiratory follow-up chest imaging appointments — CT at 3 and 6 months monitors cavity behavior.
Carry a medical alert card noting diagnosis and lifelong fluconazole requirement if being treated for coccidioidal meningitis.
prognosis: Uncomplicated acute pulmonary Valley fever in immunocompetent individuals resolves in 4-6 weeks with or without antifungal therapy; full recovery occurs in over 95% and residual pulmonary nodules cause no functional limitation. Treated pulmonary disease requiring fluconazole has cure rates above 90% at 3-6 months. Disseminated non-meningeal coccidioidomycosis responds to prolonged azole therapy in 70-90% of cases, though bone lesions may require 12-24 months of treatment. The prognosis for coccidioidal meningitis is fundamentally different: without lifelong suppression, relapse with fatal meningoencephalitis is virtually universal. With continuous fluconazole, 80-90% of patients remain disease-stable, but 30-50% develop hydrocephalus requiring shunting. HIV-positive patients with disseminated disease who achieve immune reconstitution on antiretroviral therapy have outcomes approaching immunocompetent individuals, though fluconazole secondary prophylaxis is maintained until CD4 exceeds 250 cells/µL.
recoveryTimeline: Acute fever and constitutional symptoms resolve in 2-4 weeks. Eosinophilia normalizes within 6-8 weeks. Pulmonary infiltrates clear on CT over 3-6 months. Cavities may persist asymptomatically for years but typically shrink or calcify. CF titers decline to low or undetectable levels over 6-12 months of successful therapy. Bone disease radiographically improves at 6-12 months.
complications: Coccidioidal meningitis: the most feared complication, requiring lifelong fluconazole suppression; hydrocephalus and basilar vasculitis cause permanent neurological deficits in up to 30% of survivors.
Pulmonary cavity rupture into the pleural space causing pyopneumothorax — may require tube thoracostomy and surgical repair for bronchopleural fistula.
Musculoskeletal dissemination with bone osteomyelitis and septic arthritis — spinal involvement can cause cord compression and paraplegia without urgent surgical decompression.

Relapse after premature antifungal discontinuation: CF titers rise and symptoms recur within weeks to months of stopping therapy; never discontinue fluconazole in meningeal disease without specialist approval.
Pregnancy-associated dissemination with maternal mortality up to 40% in historical series — requires aggressive antifungal management and obstetric co-management.
Adrenal insufficiency from bilateral adrenal dissemination — detected by low morning cortisol in a patient with unexplained hypotension or hyponatremia.

supportResources.name: Valley Fever Center for Excellence — University of Arizona
supportResources.description: The leading US center for Valley fever research, education, and clinical expertise. Provides diagnostic guidance and specialist referral resources.

supportResources.name: CDC Valley Fever Information
supportResources.description: CDC patient-facing pages on Valley fever symptoms, risk, testing, treatment, and prevention.

supportResources.name: IDSA Coccidioidomycosis Practice Guidelines 2016
supportResources.description: Official IDSA treatment guidelines for pulmonary and disseminated coccidioidomycosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Bacterial Pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Bacterial pneumonia responds to antibiotics within 48-72 hours; Valley fever does not. Eosinophilia is present in 40-70% of Valley fever cases but is not a feature of typical bacterial pneumonia. Exposure history to the US Southwest is the key discriminator — Coccidioides serology and antigen testing should be ordered before antibiotic re-trials in endemic area residents.

confusedWithConditions.name: Histoplasmosis (acute pulmonary)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause dimorphic fungal pneumonia with hilar adenopathy and eosinophilia, but Valley fever is geographically restricted to the arid US Southwest, while histoplasmosis is endemic to the Midwest's moist river valleys. Valley fever CF titers are a specific diagnostic tool; histoplasmosis is best diagnosed by urine antigen. Fluconazole is preferred for Valley fever; itraconazole for histoplasmosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: TB and Valley fever both cause cavitary upper-lobe lung disease with constitutional symptoms and lymphadenopathy. TB is diagnosed by acid-fast sputum smear and culture; Valley fever by serology and Coccidioides culture. Eosinophilia strongly favors a fungal etiology over tuberculosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sarcoidosis of lung
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both can present with bilateral hilar adenopathy, erythema nodosum, and arthralgias. Sarcoidosis is non-infectious and does not respond to antifungals; Valley fever does not respond to corticosteroids used in sarcoidosis. Coccidioides serology, BAL culture, and biopsy showing spherules versus non-caseating granulomas distinguish the two.

relatedConditions.name: Histoplasmosis (acute pulmonary)
relatedConditions.relevance: Sister North American endemic mycosis with similar presentation and management; distinguished by geography and specific antigen/serology tests.

relatedConditions.name: Blastomycosis (acute pulmonary)
relatedConditions.relevance: Third member of the classic North American endemic mycosis triad; all three share dimorphic fungal biology and similar antifungal management principles.

relatedConditions.name: Aspergillosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another inhaled fungal pneumonia requiring azole therapy; particularly important as a co-infection risk in severely immunocompromised patients.

relatedConditions.name: Sarcoidosis of lung
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequent diagnostic confusion due to overlapping hilar adenopathy and erythema nodosum; critical to distinguish because corticosteroid therapy for sarcoidosis would worsen Valley fever.

faqs.question: What is Valley fever?
faqs.answer: Valley fever is the common name for coccidioidomycosis, a fungal infection caused by Coccidioides immitis or Coccidioides posadasii. These fungi live in desert soils of the US Southwest (especially Arizona and California's Central Valley). Inhaling disturbed soil or dust particles transmits the infection, which typically causes flu-like respiratory illness.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of Valley fever?
faqs.answer: Symptoms appear 1-3 weeks after exposure and include fever, dry or productive cough, fatigue, joint pain, and headache. A blood test often shows elevated eosinophils. Tender red nodules on the shins (erythema nodosum) occur in 5-15% of cases. Severe disease causes shortness of breath, chest pain, and rarely stiff neck from meningitis.

faqs.question: How do you get Valley fever?
faqs.answer: Valley fever is acquired by inhaling microscopic fungal spores from disturbed desert soil. Construction, farming, outdoor recreation, and dust storms in Arizona, California's Central Valley, and northern Mexico are the most common exposure settings. It does not spread from person to person.

faqs.question: Does Valley fever go away without treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes, in most healthy adults. More than 90% of immunocompetent individuals recover without antifungal treatment within 4-6 weeks. Treatment is reserved for severe or prolonged illness, immunocompromised patients, and those at high risk of dissemination such as pregnant women, Filipinos, and African Americans.

faqs.question: What antibiotic treats Valley fever?
faqs.answer: Valley fever is a fungal infection and does not respond to antibiotics. Oral fluconazole 400 mg daily is the preferred antifungal for most pulmonary and non-meningeal disease. Itraconazole is an alternative. Severe cases require intravenous liposomal amphotericin B. Coccidioidal meningitis requires lifelong fluconazole.

faqs.question: How is Valley fever diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Valley fever is diagnosed by blood serology — IgM immunodiffusion becomes positive 1-3 weeks after illness onset and IgG complement fixation titer correlates with disease severity. Urine antigen testing and sputum culture are used in severe or immunocompromised cases. Chest CT shows characteristic infiltrates, adenopathy, or cavities.

faqs.question: Can Valley fever spread to the brain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Coccidioidal meningitis occurs in roughly 0.5% of all infections and is the most dangerous complication. It causes severe headache, neck stiffness, and altered mental status. Without treatment it is nearly always fatal within 2 years. Treatment requires lifelong fluconazole — stopping therapy invariably leads to fatal relapse.

faqs.question: Who is at highest risk of severe Valley fever?
faqs.answer: People with HIV (especially CD4 below 250), solid-organ transplant recipients, Filipinos (10-175× higher dissemination rate), African Americans, pregnant women in the third trimester, people taking anti-TNF medications, and diabetics are at the highest risk of severe or disseminated Valley fever.

faqs.question: How long does Valley fever treatment last?
faqs.answer: Mild symptomatic Valley fever requires 3-6 months of fluconazole. Disseminated bone or joint disease needs 12-24 months or longer. Coccidioidal meningitis requires lifelong fluconazole with no safe stopping point — all documented attempts to stop have resulted in fatal relapse.

faqs.question: Is Valley fever common in Arizona?
faqs.answer: Yes. Arizona accounted for approximately 75% of all 2021 US reported Valley fever cases, with Maricopa County (Phoenix area) reporting the highest absolute case numbers nationally. The CDC estimate true incidence in Arizona at 150,000-200,000 infections per year when asymptomatic cases are included.

faqs.question: Can Valley fever be prevented?
faqs.answer: There is no approved human vaccine for Valley fever. Risk reduction involves avoiding dust storms and outdoor soil work during dry seasons in endemic areas, wearing N-95 respirators during construction or landscaping activities, and avoiding high-risk environments in immunocompromised patients.

faqs.question: Can children get Valley fever?
faqs.answer: Yes, children of all ages can acquire Valley fever from the same soil exposure as adults. School-age children in Arizona have seroprevalence rates of 10-30%. Children generally have milder disease than adults, but infants and immunocompromised children can develop severe or disseminated forms requiring treatment.

faqs.question: What does eosinophilia mean in Valley fever?
faqs.answer: Eosinophilia (elevated eosinophil count above 500 per microliter) is present in 40-70% of acute Valley fever cases and is one of the most important clues that distinguishes it from bacterial pneumonia. It reflects the TH2 immune component of the response to coccidioidal antigens. Severe eosinophilia above 2,000 is highly suggestive.

faqs.question: Can Valley fever recur after treatment?
faqs.answer: Recurrence after completing a full treatment course for non-meningeal disease is uncommon (under 5%) in immunocompetent patients without further exposure. In immunocompromised patients and those with meningeal disease, relapse is nearly universal if antifungal therapy is stopped. CF titer monitoring detects early relapse.

faqs.question: Is Valley fever the same as a fungal lung infection from bird droppings?
faqs.answer: No. Valley fever is caused by Coccidioides species from desert soil — not from bird or bat droppings. Histoplasmosis is the fungal pneumonia associated with bird and bat droppings. Both cause similar flu-like respiratory illness, but their geography, diagnostic tests, and preferred antifungals differ.

simpleName: Valley fever (desert rheumatism, San Joaquin Valley fever)
symptomKeywords: pneumonia not responding to antibiotics desert
